So, that is the second reason. So, what we'll do is we'll go back up here and we'll cover these last three reasons. That way, it can give you more of the context. So, go here to the daily. So, the third one is more consistent compounding. This one I don't even have to show you an example on the charts because it's basic it's basically a math defense of this. So more consistent compounding, look at it this way. If you're going to be trying to compound your account, what that means is as your account grows, you start risking higher dollar amounts and it could be the same percentage, right? So if you start risking say 10% of $1,000 account, that's $100 per trade. Well, let's say you grow the account to $2,000, but you're still risking 10%. Even though the percentages stay the same, the dollar amount you're risking is now $200 on a $2,000 account, 10% of the account. And you're able to do this in a lot more linear of a way with less kind of volatility spikes in your equity curve as it hopefully goes up and to the right. You're able to do this way better and way more consistently with a 1:1 riskreward ratio as compared to having to brave the storm and fight in the trenches of using like a 1:2 or 1:5 or god forbid an even higher risk-to-reward ratio. With more consistent compounding, what it can do is it allows you to be able to allocate more risk to trades that are higher quality, knowing that you don't have to be a hero and catch the next crazy move of like a 1 to 10 riskreward ratio to be able to make any money. You have less losses. You have way more wins. You're not trying to quote be a hero and trying to catch the next big crazy move. You're trying to just catch the small consistent moves where price just bounces away from the major support resistance levels of a zone. Um, and I find this so much more easy and consistent to compound just because it allows you to be able to continue to scale up your risk in terms of dollar amounts even if you keep the percentage risk the same. And that in my opinion and in my experience having done this myself for years and also having taught like over 4,600 clients how to do this, I find it's a way more consistent way of growing your account in an actual way where it's like you can call it compounding. It's an actual, you know, you're not you're not growing a $100 account only $20 profit for the whole year, right? That's 20% gain. That's great. But most people whenever you're trading something like forex, you're not looking to get S&P 500 returns. You'd like to kind of outpace the market. Um, and you're only able to do that with higher risk. And you can add that higher risk through compounding. And it's easier to do with a 1:1 reward ratio than a 1:2 or or higher. Uh, number four, there's a lower probability of almost winning. So this kind of plays into number two. But this idea is that um the lower probability of almost winning means that whenever you buy say at a support zone, which for example I'm long on GBPC CAD right now, right? These are my two trades. I'm in um I'm in a USDJPY short and a GBP CAD long right now. So right this GBP CAD trade has a $50,000 profit target. Um and this one is, you know, it's if I if I win, it'll be a $50,000 profit and that would be super super nice. And the reason here that I say that there's a lower probability of almost winning is because if you're just using one to one risk-reward ratios, whenever price does start moving into profit, you'll generally get your targets hit pretty easily, right? Meaning like if you enter a trade, price just has to bounce off the zone and go into a nice significant profit. And if it goes into a significant profit of just 1 to1 riskreward ratio, those targets are way more easy to hit, right? So you have less chance of let's say you target the highs up here and it's over a 1:2 riskreward ratio. You have something like this where it can go up comes all the way back. This takes like a week and now you're still at break even. Then it tries to go up here and then it reaches really high almost hits the highs then it drops all the way back. I don't like having long trade durations and I don't like having trades that almost win, right? They almost went to the profit target. They went into significant profit and then god forbid you added to it and then it comes back and then it's a break even on your, you know, on your initial trade and your second entry that you added is now sitting on a loss and you have to close it and what could have been a nice winning trade. Have you had you just risked the higher dollar amount and taken the smaller more predictable move of just a 1:1 riskreward ratio, you're now having trades almost hit your profit target and then immediately reversing. I don't like that. I don't prefer that to be the case. And so in my opinion, one to one riskreward ratios just works way better for me. So closing it up here on the last part, I think it's better for psychology and discipline. This kind of ties together the previous four things. Whenever you're trading, you'll notice that one of the biggest detriments to your confidence is just losing streaks, right? So just losing a couple trades in a row, whether that's two, three, four, five trades in a row. That can cause a lot of people to go really, really crazy. However, if you're using a 1:1 riskreward ratio, you know, it's very likely that regularly you're going to be having winning trades, right? Because again, even if you just blindfold yourself and enter random trades with 1:1 riskreward ratio and no dynamic management, meaning no closing losing trades early, no adding to winning trades, nothing like that. You just blindfold yourself, enter 1:1 riskward ratio trades, and let it hit stop or target, you're already ahead of 90% of traders because 90% of traders lose money. If you do that, you're going to roughly break even net of fees and commissions and swaps, right? Because if you enter one to one, half the time it'll go to the profit target, the other half the time it'll go to the stop- loss. So guess what? In terms of being a better first psychology and discipline, you are starting out with a with a trading approach in which you're I don't want to say guaranteed to not lose money because you can't guarantee anything in trading. Trading is risky and the fact is is that most people lose money. It's really hard. It's really difficult to do. And so I think it's better for psychology and discipline because you know that at least half the time roughly on average given enough trades price will hit the profit target. That's a great place to start especially for a beginner. You know if if you're in a position where you're not yet profitable um you know I'd say you know you're you're beginner if you're not profitable. You're intermediate if you are somewhat break even. And then you are I don't I don't know if I'd say expert but you're doing very well if you can make any significant amount of profit over the course of like a 6 to 12 month period. Whenever you're doing that, 1:1 risk-reward ratios enable you to do that much much easier. Um, and it's way easier on your psychology because whenever you start with 1:1 risk-reward ratios and just let it play out, if you can just start closing losing trades a little earlier and then holding winning trades to the profit target and then in some cases adding to winning trades, if the context makes sense, then what you can do is you can start making a little more than you lose long term. Using one to one riskreward ratios gives you not only the freedom and dynamics to be able to manage those things and get out of losing trades early and hold winning trades longer, but it also allows you to have way more wins because you know that the next win is just right around the corner instead of having to think like, man, I've lost five trades in a row and I'm using a 1:5 riskreward ratio. And so then the likelihood of the next trade hitting that 1 to5 riskreward ratio and then the amount of time it's going to take to actually go all the way there, you know, that can be very unnerving to a lot of people. And like I don't personally like doing that. I've been trading literally like in 5 days from now it will be exactly one decade since I've been trading. I've not found anything else more easy and more simple than just using a 1:1 risk-reward ratio. And part of the reason it's so easy is because in my opinion I I have to say this subjectively, but I think it's objectively true that it is better for psychology and discipline if you're using a 1:1 risk-reward ratio because you can manage losing trades but getting out of them early and then you can hold winning trades longer thus increasing your average win making it bigger than your average loss. So that even with a 50% success rate, you're still net profitable. That is something that's so so beautiful. So because of these things, I think a 1:1 riskward ratio is definitely the way to go. You're free to experiment it. Um you're free to experiment with it for yourself. You're free to follow my trades here in Telegram to, you know, kind of like watch me apply this every day. I share basically all my trades and analysis in here and it's free. It's just a free resource I run here in addition to my YouTube channel to be able to help you learn how to trade. So I hope this video was helpful. Go test this out for yourself. Let me know what you think below in the comments. Cheers.

7X_uP9srJIc.text: This is a question that used to haunt me towards the beginning of my trading journey. And it was, "What do you do when two similar pairs have identical setups?" So, for example, I'm going to give you an example right here in which CAD JPY and ADJPY, they both got similar setups, right? They're both there at the support zones here on the daily and weekly charts. And I do think that price is in fact going to maybe attempt to bounce to the upside. So, they're two perfectly viable trades, right? I could take a buy right here at the support zone on odd JPY with a stop below and a target above or I can do a buy on KA JPY with a stop below and a target above. Now, I'm not one to predict because no one can in fact predict what's going to happen. But what I do know is that if price is right here at the zone, it gives me a good risk structure. But if I were to take both trades, it would then be doubling my risk and that's exposing me to currency correlation because if KJPY starts going down and breaking the zone and goes to the stop-loss, ADJPY is likely to do the same thing. So here's the way I approach it. The simple way I do this is I just pick what I believe is the best trading opportunity. Now I know this is subjective and it requires experience and intuition and price action discretion and context and things such as that. I'm not one to hide that. However, what I do believe is that when you look at the charts, you can get a little bit more information to help you pick what the best setup is in fact is. So whenever I look at odd JPY prices come back into this zone and sure it's a nice little zone. Yes. However, when we go to KA JPY, it does look like we have more interactions here at this zone that make it just a little bit more clear than ADJPY. So, for example, prices bounced at resistance twice. It broke above it bounced as support and then again as support recently um about 2 weeks ago and now we're back here at support again. I think CAD JPY is nicer just because the move we had off this zone was significantly longer outlived for about 300 pips. Whereas on odd JPY when price came here into this zone, yes, we had a bounce but relatively it just wasn't as far. It was 263 pips. it did go in and make a new high. And me, even in the wake of being average, I have made millions of dollars in forex and stocks and crypto consistently and predictably over the past decade since I dropped out of college. What I'm going to do in this video is I'm going to show you the differences between the way that you are trying to trade right now and the way that I currently trade. And I should be one to talk because for the past eight years, I have had a free Telegram channel where I have consistently called out all my analysis, my insights, my trades. I know what I'm talking about and I am uniquely qualified to tell you what I think works because so many of the things that I see here on YouTube that people are trying to teach you how to trade in every single video that you click on do not work because if they did, you would be profitable and you wouldn't be here right now. I'm going to cut straight to the point. I'm going to break it down very cleanly and simply for you. And what I'll also say is that if you want to join this free Telegram channel, I have a link in the description that has my free training. Click that link, put in your name and email on my website. I'll give you a one-hour long free training course about exactly how I trade in complete detail. And then in the next module in that free trading course, you can get a link to join this free Telegram channel with 55,000 members that I've been running here for the past 8 years. But what we're going to focus on here in today's video is we're going to be talking about these four things. I'm going to teach you how to completely decomplicate your trading approach and how to keep it boring. I'm then going to teach you how to structure the risk. I'm going to teach you how to do this consistently even through the ups and downs where you have basically no emotion and you can do this like a remorseless psychopath and then we're going to go through back testing examples showing this step by step. The things that I'm teaching you here in this video are very important. They are not things that I have just made up. These are things that I have actually practiced and preached for the past decade that I have been trading and they work and you can you can see that for yourself by scrolling through my whole Telegram channel after you do my free training at the link in the description after you finish this video. So, first let's talk about how to keep things boring. One of the most important rules inside my trading success comes from the idea that I barely do anything on the charts. I do not want to sit here every single day and play the types of stupid games that so many people play. I see so many people here on YouTube who are traders claiming to know what the hell they're doing that are doing so many things that don't matter and they're doing all these stupid things at the cost of things that really do matter. So, here's what I do. I use black and white charts. I use a black background and white candlesticks here on my charts. And the only thing I use is support and resistance zones. That is it. I do not use anything else. I don't use any technical indicators. I don't have any complicated institutional order block wagu beef bullish candlestick entry strategy. I don't use any fancy shiny indicators or any algorithmic trading tools. Nothing. All I do is I just use support and resistance. Here's how I trade. Whenever price gets to a clear support or resistance zone, I sell if it's at resistance and then I buy if it's at support. And that is literally all I do. I use the higher time frames like the daily and the weekly to find trades because price tends to bounce at zones on those higher time frames a lot easier than it does on the lower time frames like the 1 minute for example. And doing this I'm able to get plenty of trading opportunities and I'm usually not in trades for more than a couple days at a time. Sometimes my trade will last a week or maybe a little bit more, but most of the time I'm out of trades within a oneweek period. And that is because of the way that I approach it. I don't try to be a hero and catch the next thousand pip move with a tiny stop-loss. That's stupid. What I do is I just use simple 1:1 riskreward ratios. Basically, when price gets to a support zone, I buy with a stop below the zone and a target of a 1:1 riskreward ratio. And the same thing for resistance. Vice versa. Price gets to a resistance zone, I enter a sell. I put a stop loss above the above the zone and I risk an amount that I'm comfortable risking. Right now, I'm risking anywhere from $25,000 to $50,000 per trade with my trading capital. And then I set a one to one profit target and it either works or it doesn't. I'm going to show you all of that right here in this video. That is the way that I keep it super super boring. And what you'll often find in life, not just in trading, but in life in general, is that the boring stuff is usually the things that work. The things that you can do predictably and consistently works a million times better than trying to have all these complicated, ridiculous, nuanced trading strategies with a million different decision points and all this subjectivity. I hate that. I detest that. I can't stand that and I'm not going to sit here with a straight face and tell you that that's the way you should trade because I think that's stupid. So, when I go to the chart, all I do is I look at current market price and then I see where the next clear zone above price is and the next clear zone below price is. And then I set two alerts. One at the next resistance zone above price and one at the next support zone below price. And the moment price gets to that zone, I'll check the chart. I'll make sure there's no crazy news event causing price to instantly spike through the zone. And provided everything still looks steady state, I simply buy or sell. Now, let's talk about structuring the risk. Structuring the risk is very important because it means you're never going to lose all your money on one trade. How do I structure risk? It's basically like this. Price gets into a support zone. As a matter of fact, let me just I'm going to show you this trade I'm in right now and how I structured this trade. If it starts going into profit relatively quickly, I will just simply hold the trade. I'll try to get out a break even, a small loss, a small profit, and just call it a day. If it breaks below the zone, sometimes I'll close halfway to the stop-loss. What this does is it keeps my average loss smaller than my average win. I do not do a 1:2 risk-to-reward ratio or a 1:3 or 1:4 riskreward ratio because this does not give you any flexibility to manage the trade. Whether you're trading forex or stocks or crypto, you have to be able to get this experience. Otherwise, it's just not going to work.
